another factor which is um how companies plan their budget and and overall a timing issue, right?

to jump into oceans where there's a ton of other competitors all selling the same thing to the same

SEO offer. Let's target online marketers with my content or video editing offer. Everybody's trying

not saying don't launch those things. What I'm saying is find a way to differentiate yourself from
everybody else doing the same thing. And a lot of times the easiest way to differentiate yourself is
to find the one thing in your offer that that person actually wants. So maybe you're selling SEO
agency services. Uh there's probably a lot of people on this call trying to do something just like

the fundamentals are true, you want to target decision makers because it's much easier to sell to
them. You want to do that with an offer that you can sell high ticket with monthly recurring
revenue, and you want to sell to somebody that can afford your services. Uh, so the end result of
that is everybody's selling agency services or high ticket products to the same groups of people.
And there's nothing wrong with that. My call to action to you beginners is find a way to set
yourself apart from the pack by talking to your audience and testing different positionings that
you're not just another, you know, price card in a pile of everyone else selling the same thing, but
it's actually something that gets them excited to read that they haven't seen before. because if
they're excited and curious, they'll respond and open the door for you to go close. So, your

really well. We're delivering almost 100%. No links or tracking links of any kind in that first
email. And this is something I used to teach the opposite a year ago. This has changed dramatically.
We're not tracking open rates. Instead, we're using reply rates as a proxy for open rates. uh which
can be a challenge for sure because now I don't know really which subject line and first line of the
email is going to get the best open rates which sucks because I used to rely on that. Uh but now I'm
just looking at reply rates as a proxy for those open rates. No open rate tracking, no unsubscribe
links unfortunately where and be very careful with the words and phrases that you use in your
emails. Uh even the word un unsubscribe or opt out. Um, these are all words and phrases that we like
to avoid. That first email that we send, people shouldn't know if it's from someone trying to sell
me something or a vendor or a customer or a partner uh until they open the email. Uh, no images, no
links. Now, once you get into the inbox with that first email, I think you have a lot more leeway
for the second and third email because typically the Gmail, if say you're sitting in somebody's
inbox and a follow-up email comes, I've seen a lot less that Gmail is going to take that whole
thread and move it to spam, uh, a lot of times they'll just, you know, put it right in and this the
same thread as that email that already hit the inbox. So if you want to send a link, send a high
authority link, a YouTube link, a Loom link, a Calendarly link. Uh only in the second or third

Yeah, no worries. Take your time. Take your time. Um, okay. So, this question from Andrew um is
about your favorite top three copyrightiting frameworks um to see what's compelling best. But maybe
like we can make it a bit more broad, right? So is there anything that you're seeing is working um
for copywriting for you like any strategies? Um do you have any frameworks that work? Because I know
that will be different depending on you know from client to client. So yeah what is it that you're
using on your copywriting? Um what is your strategy there? Strategy with copywriting, you really
want to get somebody to take three separate actions when they're reading your cold email. You need
to get them to open the email, then read the email, and then reply to the email. So, I like to think
about breaking my cold emails into those three sections. How do you get someone to open the email?
That's really the subject line and the first sentence of the email. And the goal is to not let them
know that a pitch or a sales email is coming. Uh, so don't totally bait them with something
irrelevant, but keep it vague enough to peique their curiosity and get them to open it. And a lot of
times this is being creative and testing a bunch of stuff. Now a good example of that we used to use
quick question. Doesn't work anymore because it's been overplayed. But the theory of that is people
have the instinct, the psychological instinct to respond to questions. So if you make them think
that there's somebody in my inbox with a genuine question, it could be from a customer, from a
partner, from a vendor. I should open that email. So, the first thing somebody sees in their inbox
is the subject line or the first sentence of that email. The goal with those should be to throw in a
little bit of a pattern interrupt and peak their curiosity and make them think, "I should open this
email." Do not signal that you're about to sell them something. Uh, it'll kill your open rates. Then
you've got to get them to read the email. That this is where social proof and problem come in. So
really short three to four lines tops. You want to call out their problem in a really non- sales
non-promotional way and then build up some social proof ideally in a way in something that they
recognize. So if you've worked with a client they know if you've been featured somewhere that
they're aware of that will get them to immediately trust and resonate with you because they know
that person and then hopefully identify with the problem that you're calling out. Uh, so you've got
two to three sentences to make that happen. And then the last thing you want them to do is take some
sort of action. In my perfect world, you just want them to say yes or sure. So they should be able
to respond with one hand and you should not make them think. So it's can I send over some more info?
Uh, this does something you're going through. Something where they can just reply yes. Once they
reply yes, that's them raising their hand and asking for more information. Once they raise their
hand and ask them for more information, it's on you to convert them into a call. But now that you
now you know they have that problem and that that's your opening. So I don't know if that's a
framework per se. There's a lot of different ways I I I spin that to get the same result, but I
think that's the best way to think about cold email. You need to get them to open it, read it, and
say yes. Love it. Amazing. 100% agree with everything you said. Like mic drop, nothing to add there.
